title: Arrays
description: Arrays through a DSA lens: the complexity of each operation, in-place modification, and why insertion/deletion cost O(n).
objectives: Know the time complexity of access, search, insertion, and deletion on an array
Modify an array in place without extra storage
Understand why insertion/deletion at an arbitrary position costs O(n)
Recognize array traversal as the base pattern for many DSA techniques

sections.text: The earlier Arrays topic covered declaring, indexing, and looping. Now, with Time Complexity in hand, let's look at arrays the way DSA problems actually use them.

rows: Direct jump to the memory address — recall Memory Addresses' spacing formula
Must check elements one by one in the worst case
No shifting needed (for a vector with room to grow)
Every element after that position must shift over
Every element after that position must shift back

sections.text: An in-place algorithm modifies its input directly, using O(1) extra space, instead of building a new separate result.

Recall Pass by Reference: numbers is passed as &numbers, so doubleInPlace modifies the caller's actual array, not a copy. In-place techniques are extremely common in DSA precisely because they achieve the goal with O(1) extra space, connecting directly to Space Complexity.
4. A Classic Pattern: Finding the Maximum

This is O(n) time (must check every element in the worst case) and O(1) space (just one extra variable, maxVal) — the base shape behind an enormous number of array DSA problems: track a running answer while making a single pass.

Recall vector's insert()/erase(): removing an element from the middle isn't free — every element after it must shift to close the gap, an O(n) operation in the worst case. This is the direct trade-off list solved differently, at the cost of losing indexed access.

sections.title: Removing a book from a tightly packed shelf
sections.text: Pulling a book from the middle of a tightly packed shelf leaves a gap — every book after it has to slide over to close that gap. Pulling the very last book requires no sliding at all. That's exactly why arbitrary-position insertion/deletion is O(n) while end insertion is O(1).

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Assuming all array operations are O(1) — only direct indexed access is; search, and insertion/deletion at arbitrary positions, are O(n).
Building an unnecessary new array/vector when the problem could be solved in place with O(1) extra space.
Forgetting that finding a maximum, minimum, or sum in an unsorted array requires checking every element — there's no shortcut without additional structure or a sorted order.
Not accounting for the shifting cost of erase()/insert() at non-end positions when reasoning about an algorithm's overall time complexity.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is the time complexity of accessing an array element by index?
questions.answer: O(1) — direct access via the element's computed memory address.

questions.question: Why is inserting or deleting at an arbitrary position in an array O(n)?
questions.answer: Because every element after that position must shift to close or open the gap.

questions.question: What does 'in-place' mean, and why is it valued in DSA?
questions.answer: Modifying the input directly using O(1) extra space instead of creating a new structure — valued because it minimizes space complexity.

questions.question: What is the time and space complexity of finding the maximum value in an unsorted array?
questions.answer: O(n) time, since every element must be checked in the worst case, and O(1) space, since only one extra variable is needed to track the running maximum.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Arrays give O(1) indexed access, but search and arbitrary-position insertion/deletion cost O(n), since deletion or insertion in the middle requires shifting every element after that position. In-place algorithms, like doubling every element directly through a reference parameter, modify the input using only O(1) extra space instead of building a new structure. A classic pattern — tracking a running maximum with a single pass — is O(n) time and O(1) space, and this single-pass, running-answer shape underlies a large fraction of array-based DSA problems.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Arrays (DSA)
sections.text: Index access = O(1), grab any book instantly. Middle insert/delete = O(n), everyone else slides over. In-place = solve it without building a second shelf.
